title: Idioms & Fixed Expressions
description: Understand and use common English idioms to sound more natural and grasp native-speaker humour.

items.example: Break a leg tonight — I know you'll be amazing!

items.front: cost an arm and a leg
items.example: That designer bag costs an arm and a leg — I could never afford it.

items.front: hit the nail on the head
items.back: say or do exactly the right thing
items.example: You hit the nail on the head — that's exactly the problem.
I'm feeling a bit under the weather today — I think I'm getting a cold.
She only visits once in a blue moon — we barely see her.

items.front: let the cat out of the bag
items.example: He let the cat out of the bag about the surprise party.

items.back: endure a painful situation / do something difficult
items.example: I'd been avoiding the dentist, but finally I bit the bullet and made an appointment.

items.front: the ball is in your court
items.back: it is your turn to take action / your decision now
items.example: I've done everything I can. The ball is in your court now.

items.back: it's better to do something late than not at all
items.example: He finally learned to drive at 50 — better late than never!

items.prompt: She's feeling under the weather today. What does this mean?
items.explanation: 'Under the weather' = slightly ill. She's not seriously sick, just a bit off.

items.prompt: That restaurant costs an arm and a leg. What does this mean?
options: It's very painful to eat there
items.explanation: 'Costs an arm and a leg' = very expensive. You'd practically give your limbs to pay for it.

items.prompt: He let the cat out of the bag about the promotion. What happened?
items.explanation: 'Let the cat out of the bag' = reveal a secret by mistake. He accidentally told people about it.

items.prompt: I've sent my application. The ball is in their court now. What does this mean?
options: It's now their turn to act
items.explanation: 'The ball is in your court' = it's your turn to act / your decision. I've done my part.

items.prompt: Your friend is performing in a play tonight. What do you say to wish them luck?
items.explanation: 'Break a leg' is the traditional way to wish someone luck before a performance — saying 'good luck' is considered bad luck in theatre!

items.sentence: She only calls ___ in a ___ moon — I haven't heard from her in months.
You hit the ___ on the ___. That's exactly what the problem is!
I've been avoiding telling him the truth, but I need to bite the ___ and do it.
He accidentally let the cat out of the ___ about the surprise party.
She finally got her driving licence at age 45. Better ___ than ___!
